name: amyloidosis
document_html: sdfdf . [Milani, 2016] sdfdsf[Jerome, 2023]Monoclonal immunoglobulin light-chain (AL) amyloidosis arises from an unregulated proliferation of plasma cells (a plasma cell dyscrasia) that leads to the overproduction of misfolded immunoglobulin light chains [1, 2]. The serum free light chain (sFLC) assay is a critical laboratory test used to detect these circulating kappa ($\kappa$) and lambda ($\lambda$) free light chains [3, 4]. Diagnostic Utility and Sensitivity When used in combination with serum and urine immunofixation electrophoresis (IFE), the sFLC assay has a sensitivity of 99% for identifying the abnormal monoclonal proteins responsible for AL amyloidosis [5-7]. This three-test combination has a 99% negative predictive value, meaning normal results effectively rule out AL amyloidosis [3, 8]. Serum protein electrophoresis alone should not be used to exclude a monoclonal protein, as it has a poor diagnostic sensitivity of less than 75% [7, 9, 10]. The sFLC test is an essential initial step in differentiating AL amyloidosis from transthyretin (ATTR) amyloidosis prior to advanced imaging or biopsies [3, 11]. This distinction is critical because 10% to 40% of patients with ATTR cardiomyopathy may also have concurrently elevated light chains suggestive of a benign condition known as monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined significance (MGUS) [7, 12].
